questions.description: The Defamation Act 2009 was never fit for purpose and requires significant revision if it is to meet the challenges of…
questions.text: The Defamation Act 2009 was never fit for purpose and requires significant revision if it is to meet the challenges of a changing society. Critically discuss this statement paying particular attention to the deficiencies of the Act and recent suggestions for reform.

questions.description: Tom agreed to meet his wife, Rachel, for lunch in town. Advise Tom if he has any action in tort against the owners of…
questions.text: Tom agreed to meet his wife, Rachel, for lunch in town. As Tom is walking down the street towards the restaurant where they are to meet, he spots Rachel in the distance. He begins to wave at her when he hears a strange sound and looks up. He then sees a large neon sign suddenly, and without warning, fall from the third floor of the building overhead Rachel. Tom shouts a warning to Rachel but knows it is too late. He closes his eyes and drops to his knees convinced Rachel has been killed when he hears the sign crash onto the pavement. People are screaming and crying in the street and all he can do is lie on the street, wailing and sick at the thought he has lost his wife. He cannot picture life without her. In shock he is completely disoriented and is taken by ambulance to hospital. While waiting on a trolley in the Emergency Room, he sees Rachel walking towards him. Convinced that he is seeing her ghost who blames him for not saving her, he tries to flee and falls off the trolley, fracturing his ankle. In fact, Rachel avoided the falling sign and was unharmed in the incident. However, Tom is now on significant medication for these traumatic events, in particular the ghostly apparition he thought he saw. Advise Tom if he has any action in tort against the owners of the building for what he has suffered.

questions.description: In a well to do suburban neighbourhood, a local supermarket has recently… Advise local residents if they have any…
questions.text: In a well to do suburban neighbourhood, a local supermarket has recently installed a large bottle deposit return recycling machine in their car park. This machine accepts recyclable cans and plastic bottles on which a deposit has been paid when purchased. When they are returned to the machine, the person is given a voucher for the amount of the deposit which can then be spent in store. It has been in operation for a number of months. However, in recent times, the machine is either out of order or rejecting cans. As a result people are just leaving them next to the machine. The store owners decided to locate small bins next to the machine for cans which were not accepted by the machine. It appears that some people have begun to use these bins as a general can recycling drop off point as well as for rejected cans. Local residents in the housing estates surrounding the supermarket are upset at a number of issues. First, many of the cans still contain sugary liquids which are attracting flies and wasps. Second there is a strong smell from these cans and finally some children are using the cans to play 'football' in the car park when the supermarket is closed, creating a racket late at night. Advise local residents if they have any action in tort law with respect to this situation.

questions.description: Critically discuss the doctrine of res ipsa locquitur in Irish law and analyse whether it retains any usefulness in…
questions.text: Critically discuss the doctrine of res ipsa locquitur in Irish law and analyse whether it retains any usefulness in modern tort law.

questions.description: Megan is a patient in Mount Mercy Hospital. Damages.
questions.text: Megan is a patient in Mount Mercy Hospital. She undergoes surgery to correct a herniated disc in her spine. Her surgeon is Alan, with over. 30 years' experience. However, Alan has been having some personal issues and was intoxicated during the surgery. He makes several mistakes resulting in complications for Megan and the hospital has accepted liability. Following the surgery, in 90% of cases Megan would have been expected to be off work for 6 weeks, suffering moderate to mild discomfort during that period but would be expected to resume a normal life and continue without any issues. For about 10% of cases, patients may continue to suffer chronic pain of a mild nature that might need occasional pain medication when it becomes severe. For Megan following the operation she now suffers significant chronic pain, which no medication appears to alleviate. She is also abnormally anxious about further injuring her back and so has assumed a sedentary lifestyle, gaining weight and is now diabetic. She has incurred about €20,000 on alterations to her home, many of which were unnecessary but which mentally Megan desired to have done. Also, Megan can never see herself going under a surgery again as she is terrified that all surgeons are drunk. Advise Megan the principles underlying an award of damages in tort law arising from these events.

questions.description: Mary, a homeowner, asked Brendan, a retired builder, to erect a solid… Negligenc.
questions.text: Mary, a homeowner, asked Brendan, a retired builder, to erect a solid boundary wall to divide her property from that of her neighbour, Alison. Mary tells Brendan that there has been tension with Alison and she wants to ensure that the wall is correctly put up. Where Mary lives is subject to high winds that have often resulted in items from Mary's garden blowing around causing damage to her garden. Mary wants the wall to stop this from happening. Brendan says he will build it as strong as he can but with climate change the storms are getting ever stronger. When the wall is completed a storm hits. The wall collapses and destroys a shed belonging to Alison. Mary believes Brendan should pay for the damage but he says that no structure can ever be fully secured from 'unusual' weather events but it was built to the highest standards in the industry. On inspection, the wall had pillars every 4 metres although there is evidence that a common practice in construction is to have pillars every 2.5 metres unless site conditions prevent that being possible. Brendan says that he had been in the industry for 40 years and that most walls are built with pillars every 4 metres and that no one puts pillars only 2.5 metres apart. Advise Alison if she has an action in negligence against Brendan.

questions.description: Critically discuss the law relating to liability for defective products…
questions.text: Critically discuss the law relating to liability for defective products and whether the current framework provides an effective and efficient mechanism for compensation for harms caused to plaintiffs.

questions.description: Laura and Lucy work together in an office. Advise Laura with respect to any tort action she may have concerning these…
questions.text: Laura and Lucy work together in an office. In the beginning, they were very friendly but a dispute arose with respect to the organisation of a conference where each blamed the other for things that went wrong. Both were told off by their manager for what had happened but each continued to believe that the other was solely to blame for what went wrong. Over the following weeks, their resentment simmered and in the end they were unable to talk to each other. Oonagh, their line manager called them to a meeting in the Boardroom and explained that this needed to be sorted. Both Lucy and Laura just stared at each other. Neither would say anything. Suddenly Oonagh slammed her fist on the table and shouted at them that no one would leave the room until the issues had been resolved. She went over to the door and appeared to lock it. This did not improve the atmosphere in the room at all and in fact made it worse. Laura got a text message and stood up, telling Oonagh that she had to leave as her daughter was sick and needed to be taken home from school. As she got up, Lucy stood up and blocked her path, saying "I have had enough of this -- you are going nowhere until this is sorted. Sit down or I will make you!" Laura was now scared and sat down. With that, Oonagh threw her notepad across the table, knocking over a glass of water onto Laura. Oonagh then stormed out of the room, which was not locked. Advise Laura with respect to any tort action she may have concerning these events.
